$comment: Module 8 - Hardware, Advantage & Outlook. One item per objective. Grading matches apps.quizzes.views.
title: Module 8 - Hardware, Advantage & Outlook

quizzes.description: DiVincenzo criteria, platform comparison, trade-offs, scale.

questions.text: The DiVincenzo criteria specify that a scalable quantum computer needs:
questions.hint: It is a five-point checklist (plus two for communication).
questions.explanation: DiVincenzo's criteria: scalable qubits, initialization to a fiducial state, coherence times long relative to gates, a universal gate set, and qubit-specific readout (plus flying-qubit interconversion for networking).

options.text: scalable well-defined qubits, initialization, long coherence vs gate time, a universal gate set, and qubit-specific measurement
only that qubits can be measured
a classical CPU and lots of RAM
exactly 50 qubits and no more

questions.text: Which pairing of platform and property is correct?
questions.hint: Each platform trades speed against coherence and connectivity.
questions.explanation: Trapped ions offer long coherence, high fidelity, and full connectivity but slow gates; superconducting circuits give fast gates and lithographic scaling at the cost of coherence and connectivity.

options.text: trapped ions: long coherence and all-to-all connectivity but slower gates; superconducting: fast gates but shorter coherence and limited connectivity
superconducting qubits have the longest coherence of any platform
photonic qubits have deterministic two-qubit gates by default
trapped ions have the fastest gates of any platform

questions.text: The key engineering trade-offs across platforms are among:
questions.hint: No platform optimizes all four at once.
questions.explanation: Platforms differ in how they balance gate speed, coherence, qubit connectivity, and manufacturability/scalability — no single technology currently wins on every axis.

options.text: gate speed, coherence time, connectivity, and scalability
only price and marketing
the color of the dilution refrigerator
programming language choice

questions.text: An honest statement of the current hardware scale is:
questions.hint: We are firmly in the NISQ era.
questions.explanation: State-of-the-art devices range from tens up to over a thousand physical qubits, with two-qubit error rates near $10^{-2}$-$10^{-3}$ and no error correction at useful scale — the gap to fault tolerance is large.

options.text: from tens up to over a thousand noisy physical qubits with two-qubit error rates around $10^{-2}$-$10^{-3}$ and no useful error correction yet
millions of error-corrected logical qubits are available today
error rates are already below $10^{-9}$
fault-tolerant machines are commercially deployed

quizzes.description: Definitions, sampling vs useful, classical pushback, proven speedups.

questions.text: The demonstrations of 'quantum advantage' to date are based on:
questions.hint: They are contrived sampling problems, not applications.
questions.explanation: Google's random-circuit sampling and photonic boson-sampling experiments target tasks chosen to be hard to simulate classically, not tasks of practical use.

options.text: sampling tasks — random circuit sampling and boson sampling
factoring 2048-bit RSA keys
solving practical optimization problems faster than any classical method
training large language models

questions.text: Why is a sampling-based advantage not the same as a useful one?
questions.explanation: Random-circuit sampling produces no answer anyone needs; a practical advantage would speed up a problem people care about (chemistry, optimization), which has not yet been shown.

options.text: the sampling task has no known application — it was chosen because it is hard to simulate, not because the answer is wanted
sampling is slower than useful computation
sampling advantage automatically solves optimization
there is no difference

questions.text: How have claimed advantage results evolved?
questions.explanation: Better classical simulation methods have shrunk or reversed several advantage claims, making 'advantage' a contested, evolving boundary rather than a settled fact.

options.text: improved classical algorithms and supercomputer use have repeatedly narrowed or overturned specific claims
every claim has stood permanently unchallenged
classical computers have been proven unable to ever compete
the claims were all withdrawn as fraudulent

questions.text: Which is the defensible statement about quantum speedups?
questions.explanation: Rigorous speedups are known for particular tasks — Shor (exponential), Grover (quadratic), Hamiltonian simulation — but blanket claims of universal exponential speedup are not supported.

options.text: provable asymptotic speedups exist for specific problems (Shor exponential, Grover quadratic, quantum simulation), while universal exponential speedups are overstated
quantum computers are exponentially faster at everything
there are no proven speedups of any kind
only Grover offers any advantage

quizzes.description: Timelines, NISQ-vs-fault-tolerance gap, post-quantum crypto, landscape.

questions.text: A realistic view of application timelines is that:
questions.explanation: Near-term devices may offer niche value, but the high-impact applications require fault-tolerant machines that are years to decades out, gated by error-correction overhead.

options.text: NISQ devices may find niche uses soon, but transformative applications (large-scale chemistry, cryptanalysis) need fault tolerance and are years-to-decades away
all major applications are already in production
quantum computers will never be useful
cryptanalysis of RSA is possible on today's hardware

questions.text: The gap between NISQ and a cryptographically relevant Shor machine is mainly:
questions.explanation: Breaking RSA needs millions of high-quality physical qubits (thousands per logical qubit via surface codes) — far beyond NISQ. The bottleneck is fault-tolerance overhead, not algorithms.

options.text: fault tolerance — thousands of physical qubits per logical qubit are needed to run Shor at scale
just a software update away
purely a funding problem with no technical barrier
nonexistent; today's devices can already run Shor at scale

questions.text: What is the practical response to the future Shor threat?
questions.hint: 'Harvest now, decrypt later' makes it urgent already.
questions.explanation: Post-quantum cryptography replaces RSA/ECC with problems believed hard for quantum computers; standardization is underway now because encrypted data captured today could be decrypted by a future Shor machine.

options.text: post-quantum cryptography — classical schemes believed hard even for quantum computers, already being standardized
waiting until a quantum computer exists before acting
abandoning all encryption
using longer RSA keys, which fully defeats Shor

questions.text: The quantum-computing field spans which areas?
questions.hint: It is a broad, multidisciplinary landscape.
questions.explanation: Careers and research span hardware, error correction, algorithms, software/compilation, and domain applications, across academia, national labs, startups, and industry.

options.text: hardware, error correction, algorithms, software/compilers, and applications — across labs, startups, and established companies
only theoretical physics
only hardware engineering
only cryptography
